title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
TCS on Foreign Remittance: What NRIs Should Know
NRIs need to transfer money to their home countries to support their families, make investments, or handle expenses. These foreign remittances are subject to tax under the Liberalised Remittance Scheme (LRS). It’s important to know about the tax laws, rates, and exemptions for the smooth transfer of funds. In this article, we’ll focus on TCS on foreign remittance, updated rates, claim credits, and how TCS charges for remittance can be minimised.
What is TCS on Foreign Remittance?
TCS, or Tax Collected at Source, is a system where the seller collects tax directly from the buyer at the time of purchase. For foreign remittance, TCS refers to the amount deducted by the bank when you transfer money abroad. This tax is not an additional cost. Deposited with the Indian Income Tax Department, it enables the government to monitor international fund transfers and ensure compliance with current tax laws. You can later claim this amount as a tax credit when filing your ITR in India to offset your tax liability.
As per Budget 2025, annual remittances above ₹10 lakhs will attract a 20% TCS on the exceeding amount. In case of medical or education-related transfer, there is no tax or a lower TCS rate.
Current Rates of TCS on Foreign Remittance
The government has brought major reforms in Tax Collected at Source on foreign remittances in the Budget 2023 and 2025. Budget 2023 saw TCS on foreign remittance being increased from 5% to 20% except for medical and educational expenses.
Budget 2025 increased the threshold for foreign money transfer from the existing ₹7 lakhs to ₹10 lakhs. The rates imposed now vary as per the purpose of payment, which means important fund transfers like educational or medical expenses are not over-taxed, while voluntary payments like tour packages and investments are imposed to a good extent. The table below shows the current rates (effective from 1st April,2025) on different types of remittances:
Apart from the above-mentioned exemptions, no TCS will be levied on international credit card spends until further notice.
Let us consider a scenario to understand foreign remittance TCS. Suppose you invest ₹15 lakhs in a foreign asset. TCS will be applicable at 20% on the excess ₹5 lakhs (beyond ₹10 lakh). This means you’ll pay ₹16 lakh in fund transfer, where ₹1 lakh is TCS.
Tax Rules for Foreign Remittances
The latest tax changes are applicable to the following transactions:
Packages for overseas trips
Online shopping for products from international platforms
Buying foreign firm shares
Acquiring foreign assets or financial products
Investing in real estate abroad
Sending gifts or money to relatives who live in another country
Moving money to a foreign bank account while settling overseas.
Importance of TCS in Life Insurance Policies
When you invest in life insurance plans like ULIPs that have partial overseas investments, redeeming the policies, such as receiving maturity benefits or surrender benefits, may fall under TCS on foreign remittance. Also, if you pay life insurance premiums from abroad, it will include TCS too. Financial planning is important here, so that these deductions do not affect liquidity and help in the smooth processing of the life insurance benefits.
Steps to Claim TCS Credit
There are some official documents that need to be reviewed in order to claim credit for the TCS deducted on foreign money transfer during the financial period. The steps to claim the TCS credit are given below:
Step 1: Collect Form 27D from the bank or authorised dealer who deducted TCS, as it proves that TCS has been collected and deposited.
Step 2: Download Form 26AS, i.e., the tax credit statement from your Income Tax e-filing portal to get hold of all TCS/TDS entries made against your PAN.
Step 3: You can access 2 more documents from the portal - AIS (Annual Information Statement) and TIS (Tax Information Statement) as supporting documents for TCS-related transactions.
Step 4: When you file ITR, you can reduce the TCS against your unpaid tax amount. In case you’ve no tax dues, you can simply ask for a reimbursement of the tax collected.
Sending Money from India to the USA Tax-Free
NRIs are given the benefit to transfer up to USD 1 million per financial year from India to the USA without incurring any tax. As per Section 206C(1G) of the Income Tax Act, no Tax Collected at Source (TCS) is applied when money is transferred from an NRO (Non-Resident Ordinary) account to an NRE (Non-Resident External) account.
This facility helps NRIs in remitting their earnings made in India through dividends, rental incomes, salaries, or business profits to their NRO account. However, to carry out these transactions, you need prior approval from the RBI to ensure that you comply with FEMA guidelines.
Note: TCS is applicable to foreign transactions carried out using a debit card or a forex card while abroad.
Sending Money from the USA to India Tax-Free
When transferring money from the USA to India, it’s wise to understand the tax implications in both countries for better financial planning.
In the USA, there is no issue in transferring an amount up to $16,000 per person. However, above that, IRS filing is mandatory.
In India, gifts received from relatives are normally tax-free. But if the money comes from a non-relative and the amount is more than ₹50,000 in a financial year, it will be taxed.
It’s best to take the help of a tax professional for a smooth fund transfer that abides by the respective tax regulations.
Tips to Reduce Taxes on Foreign Remittances
Foreign remittance TCS, though refundable, can block your funds until the time you file your tax return. To ensure that your outflows are not affected, here are some practical tips that can be followed to keep TCS liability to a minimum:
Avoid bundled tour plans
The highest rate of 20% TCS is imposed on overseas tour packages above ₹10 lakhs. To reduce this cost, it’s better to book flights and hotels separately from their respective websites instead of opting for a combined tour plan.
Use international credit cards
Currently, transactions carried out via international credit cards are exempt from tax. So, use the cards when abroad to minimise tax liability.
Choose an education loan for overseas studies
For funding education abroad, it’s best to go for an education loan provided by an authorised financial institution, as it attracts no TCS. Further, the interest paid on these loans is deductible under Section 80E of the Income Tax Act.
Adjust TCS while filing ITR
Banks deduct TCS at the time of fund transfer and issue a certificate for the same. You can use this to deduct your TCS against the final tax liability when submitting returns. For example, a TCS of ₹1 lakh is charged by the bank when you remit ₹15 lakh to a relative (20% on the extra ₹5 lakh over the ₹10 lakh limit). If your final tax liability is ₹2 lakh, you can adjust this money and pay ₹1 lakh as the balance.
Claim a refund even for non-taxable income
If your total income falls below the minimum taxable income, you can claim the complete TCS amount as a refund while filing your return. Although no interest is paid for blocked TCS, but it ensures that you don’t lose all the money completely.
Key Takeaway
TCS on foreign remittance is applicable under LRS for amounts above ₹10 lakh; the rate depends on the purpose.
Educational and medical expenses enjoy exemptions or lower rates, while tour packages and investments attract the highest rate of 20%.
TCS credit can be adjusted against existing liability while filing returns.
Form 27D from banks, 26AS, AIS, and TIS from the Income file e-portal are needed while claiming a refund.
NRIs can transfer up to USD 1 million from their NRO to NRI accounts tax-free in a financial year after gaining RBI approval.
Some ways to reduce TCS include avoiding bundled tour packages, using an international credit card, keeping foreign fund transfers within ₹10 lakh, and funding education via loans.
Conclusion
TCS on foreign remittance ensures that money transferred abroad is transparent and in line with the government guidelines. Rates vary depending on the purpose of remittance. Proper financial planning allows you to avoid unnecessary tax outflows, adjust TCS against liability, and claim refunds. Understanding the latest TCS rates and exclusion guidelines will help you in the smooth transfer of funds and better management of overseas financial obligations.
